title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Great Silence: Reassessing the Fermi Paradox
content.subtitle: Why the apparent absence of extraterrestrial life continues to baffle the scientific community.
content.text: The universe is unimaginably vast, containing billions of galaxies, each with billions of stars and even more planets. Mathematically, the probability of life emerging elsewhere is so high that the cosmos should be teeming with civilizations. This discrepancy—the conflict between the high probability of extraterrestrial life and the total lack of evidence for it—is known as the Fermi Paradox. [31] For decades, the silence from the stars was interpreted as a sign of our own technological infancy, but as our detection methods have grown exponentially more sensitive, the 'Great Silence' has become increasingly profound and unsettling. [/31] || One school of thought suggests the existence of a 'Great Filter.' This theory posits that there is a stage in the evolutionary process that is nearly impossible to surpass. [32] The critical question for humanity is whether this filter lies in our past—perhaps the transition from simple to complex cells—or in our future, as a catastrophic event that destroys civilizations once they reach a certain level of technical prowess. [/32] If the filter is ahead of us, it implies that the 'silence' we hear is the graveyard of thousands of societies that failed to navigate the dangers of their own inventions. || [33] Another perspective, the 'Zoo Hypothesis,' proposes that advanced civilizations are aware of us but have collectively agreed to observe from a distance, much like we treat a protected nature reserve. [/33] While this appeals to our sense of cosmic importance, it relies on the assumption of a remarkably unified and disciplined galactic culture. It only takes one rogue civilization to break the silence, making this theory appear more like science fiction than a robust scientific model. || [34] More recent analyses have shifted toward the 'Aestivation Hypothesis,' which suggests that highly advanced societies may be in a state of deep sleep, waiting for the universe to cool down. [/34] Since processing information is more efficient at lower temperatures, these civilizations might be dormant, planning to wake up trillions of years in the future when the cosmic background radiation has faded. This would explain why we see no massive structures or signals; they are simply 'out of office' for the current eon. || [35] There is also the sobering possibility that we are looking for the wrong signals. We assume that an advanced race would use radio waves or lasers, but they may communicate through mediums we haven't even conceptualized yet, like dark matter manipulation or quantum entanglement. [/35] Our current search for life is like someone looking for a modern digital signal with an 18th-century carrier pigeon; the technology of the sender and receiver is fundamentally mismatched. || [36] Ultimately, the search for life elsewhere is a mirror held up to our own existence. [/36] Whether we find that we are one of many or truly alone, the answer will fundamentally alter our understanding of our place in the universe. If we are alone, our responsibility to preserve the 'spark' of consciousness on Earth becomes infinite. If we are not, we must prepare for the most significant cultural shock in human history.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author suggests that the Fermi Paradox has become more 'unsettling' because...
options: A) the universe is actually much smaller than previously estimated.
B) our ability to find evidence has improved without producing any results.
C) mathematical models are no longer considered reliable in astronomy.
D) technology has made humanity more fearful of the unknown.
questions.explanation: The text states that despite detection methods growing 'exponentially more sensitive,' the silence remains, making the lack of evidence more significant.

questions.question: What is the primary implication of the 'Great Filter' theory if the filter lies in our future?
options: A) Complex life is extremely rare in the history of the universe.
B) Humanity has already passed the most dangerous stage of its evolution.
C) Technological advancement might lead to the inevitable downfall of a species.
D) Civilizations are being destroyed by an external, unknown predator.
questions.explanation: The text describes the future filter as a 'catastrophic event' for civilizations that reach a certain 'technical prowess,' suggesting our own inventions might destroy us.

questions.question: Why does the author cast doubt on the 'Zoo Hypothesis'?
options: A) It assumes that all alien civilizations behave in a perfectly coordinated way.
B) It suggests that humans are too primitive to be observed.
C) It has been proven that nature reserves do not exist on other planets.
D) Science fiction is not a popular genre among modern astronomers.
questions.explanation: The author argues it relies on an 'assumption of a remarkably unified and disciplined galactic culture' and that one 'rogue' would ruin the plan.

questions.question: The 'Aestivation Hypothesis' explains the lack of alien signals by suggesting that...
options: A) aliens are currently traveling to other galaxies.
B) processing energy is too expensive in the current state of the universe.
C) advanced races prefer to live in warmer cosmic environments.
D) civilizations have deliberately hidden their structures from our view.
questions.explanation: The text states that information processing is 'more efficient at lower temperatures,' so they are 'dormant' until the universe cools down.

questions.question: What point does the author make with the 'carrier pigeon' analogy?
options: A) Early forms of communication were more reliable than digital ones.
B) We are likely using outdated technology to look for advanced communication.
C) Dark matter is the most probable way for aliens to send messages.
D) Humans will never be able to understand extraterrestrial signals.
questions.explanation: The analogy illustrates that our current tools (like radio) might be 'fundamentally mismatched' with the advanced methods (like dark matter) used by others.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author concludes that the result of the search for life...
options: A) is irrelevant compared to the environmental crises on Earth.
B) will lead to an immediate and violent cultural collapse.
C) will force a profound shift in how we perceive our own importance.
D) depends entirely on our ability to master quantum entanglement.
questions.explanation: The text says the answer will 'fundamentally alter our understanding of our place in the universe,' emphasizing the philosophical shift in either scenario.
